name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ أَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (١٥)
```

```english
O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (15)
```

```arabic
إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٦)
```

```english
If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring about a new creation; (16)
```

```arabic
وَمَا ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ (١٧)
```

```english
and that is not a hard thing for Allah. (17)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَإِن تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَىْءٌ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰٓ ۗ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَن تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٨)
```

```english
No bearer shall bear another’s burden, and should someone heavily burdened call [another] to carry it, nothing of it will be carried [by anyone] even if he should be a near relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord in secret and maintain the prayer. Whoever purifies himself, purifies only for his own sake, and to Allah is the return. (18)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ (١٩)
```

```english
The blind one and the seer are not equal, (19)
```

```arabic
وَلَا ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَلَا ٱلنُّورُ (٢٠)
```

```english
nor darkness and light; (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَا ٱلظِّلُّ وَلَا ٱلْحَرُورُ (٢١)
```

```english
nor shade and torrid heat; (21)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَحْيَآءُ وَلَا ٱلْأَمْوَٰتُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسْمِعُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُسْمِعٍ مَّن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٢٢)
```

```english
nor are the living equal to the dead. Indeed Allah makes whomever He wishes to hear, and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear you. (22)
```

```arabic
إِنْ أَنتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
You are but a warner. (23)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا ۚ وَإِن مِّنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلَا فِيهَا نَذِيرٌ (٢٤)
```

```english
Indeed We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of good news and as a warner; and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it. (24)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَبِٱلزُّبُرِ وَبِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ (٢٥)
```

```english
If they impugn you, those before them have impugned [likewise]: their apostles brought them manifest proofs, [holy] writs, and illuminating scriptures. (25)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (٢٦)
```

```english
Then I seized the faithless. So how was My rebuttal! (26)
```

## (Commentary)

After clarifying to them that creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) belong solely to Him, the Exalted—and thus He is their Lord to Whom belongs all sovereignty (الملك), to the exclusion of those they invoke besides Him, who possess nothing and cannot even sustain their own governance—He begins to explain this through another perspective, tinged with warning (الوعيد) and threat (التهديد). This perspective is that He, the Exalted, is absolutely self-sufficient (غني) and independent of them, while they are entirely destitute (فقراء) and in need of Him. Therefore, He has the power to do away with them and bring forth a new creation if He so wills, as a recompense for what they have earned.

Then, He addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with a message whose gist is that this seizure and destruction do not encompass anyone except these deniers, excluding the believers upon whom the warning (الإنذار) of the Prophet (عليه السلام) has an effect. There is a manifest difference between the two groups. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a warner like the warners of the past, and his situation is like that of the warners before him; if they deny him, then the deniers among the nations of the past likewise denied the previous prophets, so Allah seized them with a severe seizure, and He shall likewise seize the deniers of this nation.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ** — "O mankind! It is you who are the needy (الفقراء) — *those in absolute state of want and dependence* — of Allah, and Allah is the All-sufficient (الغني) — *the One who is absolutely independent and self-subsistent* — the All-laudable (الحميد) — *the One who is inherently worthy of all praise for His perfect acts*." (35:15). There is no doubt that this verse serves as a kind of preamble for the two subsequent verses, clarifying their content, while remaining independent in its own significance.

The explanation of this is that the context suggests that the actions of these deniers revealed a delusion: they imagined they could be independent of Allah, the Glorified, by worshipping their idols, and that Allah had some need for them, which is why He calls them to Himself through the divine invitation carried out by His messengers. Thus, they perceived a state of independence (غنى) and poverty (فقر), assigning themselves a share of independence and attributing to Allah a share of poverty—exalted is He far above that.

So Allah, the Glorified, refuted their claim by saying: "O mankind! It is you who are the needy of Allah, and Allah is the All-sufficient." He restricted poverty to them and restricted independence to Himself, the Glorified. Thus, all poverty is within them, and all independence is within Him, the Glorified. Since independence and poverty—which are synonymous with possession (الوجدان) and deprivation (الفقدان)—are opposites that cannot both be absent from their subject, the previous restriction necessitates another: their restriction to poverty and His restriction to independence. Thus, they have nothing but poverty, and He, the Exalted, has nothing but independence.

Allah, the Glorified, is independent by essence (بالذات); He has the power to do away with them and be independent of them. They, however, are poor by essence; they have no way to be independent of Him through any other.

The criterion (الملاك) for His independence from them and their poverty toward Him is that He, the Exalted, is their Creator and the Governor of their affairs. This is alluded to by the use of the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) in the explanation of their poverty and His independence, and the allusion to creation and governance in His saying: "If He wishes, He will take you away and bring a new creation," as well as His being described as "the All-laudable" (الحميد), which refers to the One who is praised for His action, which is His creation and governance.

Thus, the meaning of the speech returns to something like our saying: "O mankind, insofar as you are created and governed by Allah, you are the ones in absolute need of Allah; in you is all poverty and want. And Allah, insofar as He is the Creator and Governor, is the Independent One, and there is no independence besides Him."

Based on this, there is no harm in the restriction of poverty to "mankind" (الناس), whether it refers specifically to the deniers or to humanity in general, despite the fact that other creatures are equally in need of Allah. This is because the generality of the cause (علة) for a ruling generalizes the ruling itself. It is as if it were said: "You, the assembly of creation, are the ones in need of your Creator, the Governor of your affairs, and He is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable."

Various answers have been given to the problem of restricting poverty to mankind when it is common to others as well. Among them: that the restriction of poverty to mankind is an exaggeration (مبالغة) of their poverty, as if, due to the multiplicity of their needs and the intensity of their want, they alone are the "needy," and the need of other creatures compared to theirs is as if non-existent. For this reason, the Exalted said: "Man was created weak." This does not exclude the Jinn, for they do not require food, clothing, and other things as man does.

Another answer: that the intended meaning is mankind and others, following the method of giving preponderance (تغليب) to those present over those absent, and to those possessing intellect (أولي العلم) over those without.

Another: that the definite article (اللام) in "mankind" (الناس) refers to a specific group (للعهد) and in "the needy" (الفقراء) refers to the genus (للجنس), because those addressed in the verse are the same as those addressed in His saying: "That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs the kingdom..." i.e., that worshipped One is the One described with attributes of majesty, not those you invoke besides Him, and you are the most needy of all creation toward Him.

Another: that the restriction is relative (إضافي) in relation to Him, the Exalted, not absolute (حقيقي).

It is not hidden from you that the significance of the verse and its context do not align well with any of these answers. However, the last answer can be interpreted in a way that returns to the view we previously presented.

The conclusion of the verse with the attribute "the All-laudable" (الحميد) is to indicate that He is independent and praised in His actions, whether He gives or withholds. This is because if He gives, He does not give for a return, due to His independence from recompense or gratitude—and every return is hypothetical—and if He withholds, no blame is directed toward Him, as no one has a right over Him, and nothing is possessed independently of Him.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ وَما ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ بِعَزِيزٍ** — "If He wishes, He will take you away and bring a new creation, and that is not a hard thing for Allah." (35:16-17). That is, if He wills your removal, He will remove you, O mankind, because He is independent of you and is not harmed by your departure; and He will bring a new creation who will laud Him and praise Him, not because of a need He has for them, but because He is All-laudable, and His nature necessitates that He be generous and thus praised. This is not difficult for Allah due to His absolute power, for He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

It has become clear that the content of this verse is branched (متفرع) from the content of the previous verse. His saying: "If He wishes, He will take you away" is branched from His being All-sufficient (الغني), and His saying: "and bring a new creation" is branched from His being All-laudable (الحميد). The content of these two sentences is branched in another place from His independence and His mercy; the Exalted said: "Your Lord is the All-sufficient, the Lord of mercy. If He wishes, He will take you away and cause whom He wishes to succeed you..." (6:133).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** — "No bearer shall bear another’s burden..." (35:18). Al-Raghib said: **الوزر** (with two *fathahs*) is the refuge (الملجأ) one seeks in a mountain; the Exalted said: "No! There is no refuge (وزر)." And **الوزر** (with *kasrah* then *sukun*) is the weight (الثقل), by way of comparison to the weight of a mountain. It is used to express sin (الإثم) just as it is expressed by "weight"; the Exalted said: "that they may bear their burdens (أوزارهم) in full..." similar to His saying: "They shall surely carry their own loads (أثقالهم) and other loads along with their own loads." (End of quote). Thus, the meaning is: no soul that bears a sin shall bear the sin of another soul. The necessary consequence of this is that no soul shall be taken to task except for the sin it has carried itself and the burden (الوزر) it has earned.

The verse seems to be a refutation of a potential misconception suggested by the end of the previous discourse. When He said: "If He wishes, He will take you away and bring others," and threatened them with destruction and annihilation, it might be asked: "These deniers are seized for their burden, but what is the state of the believers? Will they be seized for the burden of others?" So the answer was given that no bearer shall bear the burden of another, and no soul shall carry the load of another that has weighed it down, even if it be a kinsman.

Thus, these deniers are the ones intended by the threat, and your invitation and warning do not benefit them because their hearts have been sealed. Your warning only benefits those who fear their Lord in the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — and maintain the prayer (الصلاة). The two groups are not equal, for their likeness is that of the blind and the seeing, the darkness and the light, the shade and the scorching heat, and the living and the dead.

So His saying: "No bearer shall bear another’s burden" means no soul bearing a burden and sin shall carry the sin of another soul that is also a bearer.

And His saying: **وَإِنْ تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلى حِمْلِها لا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَيْءٌ وَلَوْ كانَ ذا قُرْبى** — "and if one weighed down calls [another] to [bear] its load, naught of it will be carried, even if it be a kinsman." That is, if a soul weighed down (مثقلة) by its load of sin calls another to carry what it bears of sin so that it might be relieved of it, it will not be answered, and nothing of its load will be carried, even if the one called is a kinsman (ذا قربى) to the caller, such as a father, mother, brother, or sister.

And His saying: **إِنَّما تُنْذِرُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ** — "You can only warn those who fear their Lord in the unseen and maintain the prayer." That is, these deniers do not benefit from the warning, and the reality of warning is not realized with them because their hearts are sealed. You only warn, and your warning only benefits, those who fear their Lord in the Unseen and maintain the prayer, which is the most virtuous and important of acts of worship; in short, those who believe in Allah and worship Him. That is, those who fear their Lord in the Unseen and maintain the prayer *as a result* of your warning, not that they fear their Lord and pray and then are warned afterward—which would imply achieving what is already achieved (تحصيل الحاصل). The verse is like His saying: "I saw myself pressing wine" (12:36) [meaning pressing grapes that will become wine].

And His saying: **وَمَنْ تَزَكَّى فَإِنَّما يَتَزَكَّى لِنَفْسِهِ** — "and whoever purifies himself, purifies himself only for his own sake." He substituted fear and the maintenance of prayer with purification (التزكي) to indicate that what is sought by the invitation and warning is purification, and the purification of the soul is its being clothed in the fear of Allah in the Unseen and the maintenance of prayer.

In this is a confirmation and emphasis of what preceded regarding His being All-sufficient and All-laudable; for He, the Exalted, does not benefit from the purification He calls toward; rather, he who purifies himself does so only for the benefit of his own soul.

He concluded the verse with His saying: **وَإِلَى اللهِ الْمَصِيرُ** — "and to Allah is the return (المصير)." This is to indicate that the purification of the one who purifies himself does not go to waste, for both groups will inevitably return to their Lord, and He will call them to account and recompense them, rewarding those who purified themselves with the best reward.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ** — "The blind and the seeing are not equal," (35:19). It appears to be conjoined to His saying: "and to Allah is the return," serving as a justification in the form of a parable for the lack of equality between these who purify themselves and those deniers. It has also been said that it is conjoined to His previous saying: "The two seas are not equal."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا الظُّلُماتُ وَلَا النُّورُ** — "nor are darkness and light," (35:20). The repetition of the negative particles (حروف النفي) time after time in this verse and what follows is to emphasize the negation.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا الظِّلُّ وَلَا الْحَرُورُ** — "nor are the shade and the scorching heat," (35:21). The scorching heat (الحرور) is the intensity of the sun's heat, according to what has been said. It is also said to be the *samum* (poisonous wind); others say the *samum* blows during the day, while the *حرور* blows by night and day.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَحْياءُ وَلَا الْأَمْواتُ** — "The living and the dead are not equal..." (35:22) until the end of the verse. This is conjoined to His saying: "The blind and the seeing are not equal." The phrase "are not equal" (ما يستوي) was repeated, rather than simply conjoining "the living and the dead" to "the blind and the seeing" as a fourth pair, because of the long separation between them. Thus, "are not equal" was repeated so that the meaning would not escape the mind of the listener. It is like His saying: "How can there be for the polytheists a treaty with Allah and His Messenger..." until He said: "How [can there be a treaty] while, if they were to prevail over you..." (9:7-8).

These successive and ordered sentences—from His saying: "The blind and the seeing are not equal" to His saying: "The living and the dead are not equal"—are parables for the believer and the disbeliever and the consequences of their deeds.

And His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُسْمِعُ مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "Indeed Allah makes whomever He wishes hear." This refers to the believer who was dead, and then Allah gave him life and made him hear because of the readiness (الاستعداد) within his soul for that. The Exalted said: "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light..." (6:122). As for the Prophet (عليه السلام), he is merely a means, and guidance (الهدى) is the guidance of Allah.

And His saying: **وَما أَنْتَ بِمُسْمِعٍ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ** — "and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear." That is, the dead; and what is meant by them are the disbelievers whose hearts have been sealed.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ أَنْتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ** — "You are but a warner." (35:23). This is a relative restriction (قصر إضافي), meaning: you have nothing but their warning; as for the guidance of those among them who are guided, and the straying of those who stray and are not guided as a recompense for their evil deeds, that belongs only to Allah, the Glorified.

He did not mention "bearer of good news" (البشير) along with "warner" (النذير), despite his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) possessing both attributes, because the context is one of warning, so it was appropriate to restrict it to the attribute of warning, even though it is mentioned in the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ بَشِيراً وَنَذِيراً وَإِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ** — "Indeed We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of good news and as a warner; and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it." (35:24). The significance, as necessitated by the context, is: "We have sent you with glad tidings and warning, and this is not an unprecedented or strange thing; for there is no nation among the nations but that a warner has passed and gone within it." This is among the established sunnahs (سنن) of Allah in His creation.

The apparent context is that "warner" (النذير) refers to a messenger (الرسول) sent from Allah. Some have interpreted "warner" as anyone who provides exhortation and warning, whether a prophet or a scholar who is not a prophet, but this is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse.

Indeed, it is not mandatory that the warner of every nation be from among its own members; for the Exalted said: "passed in it" (خلا فيها) and did not say: "passed from it" (خلا منها).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَبِالْزُّبُرِ وَبِالْكِتابِ الْمُنِيرِ** — "If they deny you, those who were before them also denied. Their apostles brought them manifest proofs, the scriptures, and the illuminating Book." (35:25). The manifest proofs (البينات) are the miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) that testify to the truthfulness of the messengers. The scriptures (الزبر) is the plural of *zabur*; perhaps what is meant by it—by virtue of its contrast with "the Book"—are the scrolls and books containing the remembrance of Allah without containing laws (الأحكام) and legislations (الشرائع). The illuminating Book (الكتاب المنير) is the Book sent down from heaven containing legislations, such as the Book of Noah and Abraham, the Torah of Moses, and the Gospel of Jesus (عليه السلام). The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ** — "Then I seized those who were faithless, and how was My rebuke!" (35:26). Seizing (الأخذ) is a metaphor for punishment, and rebuke (النكير) is denial/disavowal (الإنكار). The rest is clear.

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of the Universality of Warning)

It has already been presented in the discussions on Prophethood (النبوة) in the second volume, and in the stories of Noah (عليه السلام) in the tenth volume of this book, that which indicates through reason the universality of prophethood, and the Book supports this.

Thus, no nation among the human nations is devoid of the appearance of something of the true prophetic call within it. As for the prophet of every nation being from that same nation, there is no evidence for it; and you have seen that His saying, the Exalted: "and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it" signifies this.

As for the actuality (فعلية) of the warning—such that it reaches every single individual of the nation, in addition to the fundamental requirement (أصل الاقتضاء)—and the prevalence of the call to every single person, the governance of competing causes and factors (العلل والأسباب المتزاحمة) in this material realm (النشأة المادية) does not allow for it, just as it does not allow for other general requirements decreed by the creative process. For example, it is in the constitution of every human newborn to live a natural lifespan, yet accidents prevent most individuals from that. Every human newborn is equipped with a reproductive system for procreation, yet many individuals die before reaching maturity and thus do not achieve it, among other similar examples.

Thus, prophethood and warning are universal for every nation, but this does not necessarily entail that the call must reach every single person among them. Rather, it is possible that it reaches some of the nation directly or indirectly, while failing to reach others due to the intervention of competing causes and factors between them and its reaching them. Those toward whom the call was directed and it reached them, the proof (الحجة) is completed against them. Those toward whom it was directed but it did not reach them, the proof is not completed against them; they are among the oppressed (المستضعفين) — *those who are unable to find a way to the truth due to external constraints* — and their affair is with Allah. The Exalted said: "Except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither the resources nor are guided to any way." (4:98).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "No bearer shall bear another’s burden": Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Majah narrated from 'Amr ibn al-Ahwas: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said during the Farewell Pilgrimage: **ألا لا يجني جان إلا على نفسه ـ لا يجني والد على ولده ولا مولود على والده** — "Lo! No criminal commits a crime except against himself; no father commits a crime against his child, nor does a child against his father."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah makes whomever He wishes hear, and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear," he said: "These disbelievers do not hear from you—just as the people of the graves do not hear."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Sahl al-Sirri ibn Sahl al-Jundisaburi narrated in the fifth of his *hadith* through the path of 'Abd al-Quddus from Abu Salih from Ibn 'Abbas: regarding His saying: "Indeed you cannot make the dead hear, and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear," he said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood over the slain on the day of Badr and said: 'Have you found what your Lord promised you to be true? O so-and-so son of so-and-so, did you not disbelieve in your Lord? Did you not deny your Prophet? Did you not sever your ties of kinship?' They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do they hear what you say?' He said: 'You do not hear what I say any better than they do.' So Allah revealed: 'Indeed you cannot make the dead hear, and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear,' a parable Allah struck for the disbelievers, that they do not hear His word."

I say: In this narration, there are signs of fabrication (الوضع) that are not hidden. The station of the Prophet (عليه السلام) is too sublime for him to say something for which he has no knowledge from his Lord, such that Allah would reveal a verse to deny him in what he claims and informs.

Furthermore, what he quoted of the verse does not match the *Mus-haf* (the written Quran); its beginning is taken from Surah al-Naml, verse 80, and its end is taken from Surah Fatir, verse 22.

Moreover, the context of the verse is Meccan, within a sequence of preceding and succeeding Meccan verses [whereas Badr was Medinan].

In *al-Ihtijaj*, in the debate of Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): The questioner said: "Then tell me about the Magians (المجوس); did He send a prophet to them? For I find they have precise books, eloquent exhortations, and healing parables, and they acknowledge reward and punishment, and they have laws they act upon." He said: "There is no nation but a warner has passed in it. A prophet was indeed sent to them with a book from Allah, but they rejected him and denied his book."
